title: Transforming the Workforce: Is AI Getting Good Enough to Train Itself?
content:
Beyond the messy day-to-day mechanics of atomizing jobs into training tasks is a bigger question about AI. Are the machines actually getting good enough to do the work themselves? In the past year, the AI industry has tried to answer that question with a growing ecosystem of evaluation frameworks. Like a standardized test, an evaluation measures a language model's math ability, reasoning, or factual accuracy. However, they do not typically measure the kinds of specific, judgment-heavy tasks that white-collar workers do all day.

Mercor has developed its own test, a project called the AI Productivity Index, that attempts to do just that. It measures professional performance and posts the results online for anyone to follow as an industry leaderboard. To build this index, Mercor worked with accomplished contractors to design short professional scenarios. These problems cover fields such as law, medicine, management consulting, investment banking, and software engineering.

Experts build and review the actual test cases, defining what the correct answers should look like. The AI models then attempt the task, and their answers are graded against the rubric line by line. So far, the models often fall short of expectations. The best systems can produce useful work in certain areas, but they are not exactly reliable employees yet. Top systems still struggle on complex real-world tasks, failing to meet the production bar.

These results mirror what other attempts to measure AI's real-world impact have been finding. For example, a fictional wellness company expanding overseas requires analyzing unwieldy digital paper trails. When models are prompted to answer strategic questions and calculate costs, they often struggle with the granular details. Anthropic recently released a chart showing a striking gap between what AI systems appear capable of doing and how often they are actually used on the job.

In addition to technical hurdles, AI companies face significant security and management challenges. Mercor recently experienced a sprawling supply chain hack through a corrupted open-source developer tool. As much as four terabytes of data, including personal information, were exposed. The fallout was swift, resulting in lawsuits and paused projects as the industry grappled with the vulnerabilities of AI development.
